mnemonics.title: Remember Key Types
mnemonics.content: P-C-S-F: Primary, Candidate, Super, Foreign. Think 'Prime Candidates Serve Families' - Primary Candidates Serve as Foreign keys in other tables.

mnemonics.title: Entity vs Referential Integrity
mnemonics.content: Entity = 'Self' (Primary Key cannot be NULL). Referential = 'Relationship' (Foreign key must match or be NULL). Remember: E for Entity = Essential identity.

mnemonics.title: Degree vs Cardinality
mnemonics.content: Degree = vertical (columns), Cardinality = counting (rows). 'Degree' has 'g' like 'grid' - columns are vertical lines.

mnemonics.title: Keys Hierarchy
mnemonics.content: Super Key > Candidate Key > Primary Key. Think: Super contains Candidates, Candidates compete for Primary.

acronyms.expansion: Primary Key - Unique identifier for each tuple, cannot be NULL
acronyms.usage: In Student(SID, Name), SID is PK

acronyms.expansion: Foreign Key - References PK of another table, establishes relationship
acronyms.usage: In Orders(CustID), CustID FK references Customers(CustID)

acronyms.expansion: Candidate Key - Minimal superkey, can become Primary Key
acronyms.usage: In Employee(EmpID, Email), both are CKs; choose one as PK

acronyms.expansion: Super Key - Set of attributes that uniquely identify tuples

visualTips.visualization: Draw a table with labeled columns (attributes) and rows (tuples). Primary Key column should have a key icon (🔑) next to it.
Use concentric circles: Outer = Super Key, Middle = Candidate Key, Inner = Primary Key. Show Foreign Key as an arrow pointing to another table's PK.
Draw three pillars: Entity Integrity (🏛️ pillar for identity), Referential Integrity (🔗 linking pillar), Domain Integrity (📋 list pillar for valid values).

analogies.analogy: Like Aadhaar/UID number - uniquely identifies every citizen, never changes, cannot be null.
Like your father's surname linking you to your family tree - connects you to another family while maintaining your own identity.
Like multiple candidates competing for one job (Primary Key position). Each can do the job, but only one gets selected.
Like a class roll number (Batch + Number) - neither alone identifies the student, but together they do.
Like a person without a name/ID - cannot exist in a database of identified entities.
Like a library book reference - if you cite a book, it must exist in the library catalog.
